{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Japan}}
{{Expand Japanese|猿田彦神社}}{{infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Ise Sarutahiko Shrine.jpg | caption = Shrine | deity = [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]] }} '''Sarutahiko Shrine''' (猿田彦神社) is a [[shinto shrine]] in [[Ise, Mie]].<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.sarutahikojinja.or.jp/english/ | title=Dedicated to Sarutahiko Okami, the Kami of Guidance｜みちひらきの大神 猿田彦神社 }}</ref> located close to [[Ise Grand Shrine]].<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Teeuwen |first1=Mark |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=T9rWDQAAQBAJ&dq=Sarutahiko+Shrine&pg=PT393 |title=A Social History of the Ise Shrines: Divine Capital |last2=Breen |first2=John |date=2017-02-09 |publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing |isbn=978-1-4742-7280-3 |language=en}}</ref> It is a [[beppyo shrine]] and a [[Hokora]] in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|modern system of ranked shinto shrines]].
The priests of the shrine are direct descendants of [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Dedicated to Sarutahiko Okami, the Kami of Guidance｜みちひらきの大神 猿田彦神社 |url=https://www.sarutahikojinja.or.jp/english/ |access-date=2023-10-21 |website=www.sarutahikojinja.or.jp}}</ref> who it worships.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-22 |title=Sarutahiko {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9158 |access-date=2023-12-07 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220122143817/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9158 |archive-date=2022-01-22 }}</ref>
It is linked to [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] the [[Ichinomiya]] of [[Ise Province]].<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref><ref name="mw-2012a">{{Cite web |title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120213073802/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |archive-date=2012-02-13 |access-date=2023-12-01}}</ref><ref name=":0">Picken, Stuart. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=v7Xs1NzMMHMC&pg=PA377 ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 377]; [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 3] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf|date=2013-05-17}}; retrieved 2012-8-23.</ref> That shrine is sometimes seen as the head of all Sarutahiko shrines, but other people say this one is.
==Mythical origin== {{Shinto}} [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]] ({{lang-ja|猿田毘古大神, 猿田彦大神}}) is a deity of the [[Religion in Japan|Japanese religion]] of [[Shinto]]; he is the leader of the earthly ''[[kami]],'' the [[kunitsukami]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>
In the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', he is the one who meets [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], the grandson of [[Amaterasu]], the Sun goddess, when he descends from [[Takama-ga-hara]].<ref>{{cite web|title=English|url=http://www.sarutahiko-jinjya.net/img/panf_e.pdf|publisher=尾張猿田彦神社|access-date=14 August 2012}}</ref> He is depicted as a towering man with a large beard, jeweled spear, ruddy face, and long nose. At first he is unwilling to let Ninigi pass until persuaded by [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto]], the kami of dance and the arts, whom he later marries.<ref>{{cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.tsubakishrine.org/history/index.html|publisher=Tsubaki Grand Shrine America|access-date=13 August 2012}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Ninigi|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en}}</ref> Even Sarutahiko was regarded as the "god who illuminates the sky and the earth" in Ise, and was worshiped by local believers as the '''sun god''' (personification of sun) before [[Amaterasu]].
After guiding the [[Amatsukami]] to earth, he rested at the [[Isuzu River]] and here this shrine was established
He, Ame-no-Uzume and their children subsequently created the ''{{ill|Sarume clan|ja|猿女君}}'' (猿女の君).<ref name="picken-2004a">{{Cite book |last=Picken |first=Stuart D. B. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&q=Uzume&pg=PA1 |title=Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents |date=2004 |publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group |isbn=978-0-313-26432-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="roberts-2009a">{{Cite book |last=Roberts |first=Jeremy |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Uzume |title=Japanese Mythology A to Z |date=2009 |publisher=Infobase Publishing |isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="coulter-2013a">{{Cite book |last1=Coulter |first1=Charles Russell |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&dq=Ame-no-Uzume&pg=PT91 |title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities |last2=Turner |first2=Patricia |date=2013-07-04 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-135-96397-2 |language=en}}</ref> clan along with an order of female court and religious dancers. It was the origin of [[Kagura]] and [[Noh]]. Other descendants includes the Ujitoko clan from Ise province.
[[File:Sarutahiko Ōkami crop from Wittig collection.png|thumb|left|upright|Sarutahiko Ōkami; taken from a late-19th-century Japanese painting.]]
==See also== * [[Tengu]] * [[Garuda]] * [[Shirahige Jinja]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Japan}}

It is linked to [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] the [[Ichinomiya]] of [[Ise Province]].<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref><ref name="mw-2012a">{{Cite web |title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120213073802/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |archive-date=2012-02-13 |access-date=2023-12-01}}</ref><ref name=":0">Picken, Stuart. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=v7Xs1NzMMHMC&pg=PA377 ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 377]; [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 3] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf|date=2013-05-17}}; retrieved 2012-8-23.</ref> That shrine is sometimes seen as the head of all Sarutahiko shrines, but other people say this one is.

B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>
In the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', he is the one who meets [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], the grandson of [[Amaterasu]], the Sun goddess, when he descends from [[Takama-ga-hara]].<ref>{{cite web|title=English|url=http://www.sarutahiko-jinjya.net/img/panf_e.pdf|publisher=尾張猿田彦神社|access-date=14 August 2012}}</ref> He is depicted as a towering man with a large beard, jeweled spear, ruddy face, and long nose. {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Japan}}
{{Expand Japanese|猿田彦神社}}{{infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Ise Sarutahiko Shrine.jpg | caption = Shrine | deity = [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]] }} '''Sarutahiko Shrine''' (猿田彦神社) is a [[shinto shrine]] in [[Ise, Mie]].<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.sarutahikojinja.or.jp/english/ | title=Dedicated to Sarutahiko Okami, the Kami of Guidance｜みちひらきの大神 猿田彦神社 }}</ref> located close to [[Ise Grand Shrine]].<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Teeuwen |first1=Mark |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=T9rWDQAAQBAJ&dq=Sarutahiko+Shrine&pg=PT393 |title=A Social History of the Ise Shrines: Divine Capital |last2=Breen |first2=John |date=2017-02-09 |publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing |isbn=978-1-4742-7280-3 |language=en}}</ref> It is a [[beppyo shrine]] and a [[Hokora]] in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|modern system of ranked shinto shrines]].
The priests of the shrine are direct descendants of [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Dedicated to Sarutahiko Okami, the Kami of Guidance｜みちひらきの大神 猿田彦神社 |url=https://www.sarutahikojinja.or.jp/english/ |access-date=2023-10-21 |website=www.sarutahikojinja.or.jp}}</ref> who it worships.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-22 |title=Sarutahiko {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9158 |access-date=2023-12-07 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220122143817/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9158 |archive-date=2022-01-22 }}</ref>
It is linked to [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] the [[Ichinomiya]] of [[Ise Province]].<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref><ref name="mw-2012a">{{Cite web |title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120213073802/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |archive-date=2012-02-13 |access-date=2023-12-01}}</ref><ref name=":0">Picken, Stuart. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=v7Xs1NzMMHMC&pg=PA377 ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 377]; [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 3] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf|date=2013-05-17}}; retrieved 2012-8-23.</ref> That shrine is sometimes seen as the head of all Sarutahiko shrines, but other people say this one is.
==Mythical origin== {{Shinto}} [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]] ({{lang-ja|猿田毘古大神, 猿田彦大神}}) is a deity of the [[Religion in Japan|Japanese religion]] of [[Shinto]]; he is the leader of the earthly ''[[kami]],'' the [[kunitsukami]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>
In the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', he is the one who meets [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], the grandson of [[Amaterasu]], the Sun goddess, when he descends from [[Takama-ga-hara]].<ref>{{cite web|title=English|url=http://www.sarutahiko-jinjya.net/img/panf_e.pdf|publisher=尾張猿田彦神社|access-date=14 August 2012}}</ref> He is depicted as a towering man with a large beard, jeweled spear, ruddy face, and long nose. At first he is unwilling to let Ninigi pass until persuaded by [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto]], the kami of dance and the arts, whom he later marries.<ref>{{cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.tsubakishrine.org/history/index.html|publisher=Tsubaki Grand Shrine America|access-date=13 August 2012}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Ninigi|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en}}</ref> Even Sarutahiko was regarded as the "god who illuminates the sky and the earth" in Ise, and was worshiped by local believers as the '''sun god''' (personification of sun) before [[Amaterasu]].
After guiding the [[Amatsukami]] to earth, he rested at the [[Isuzu River]] and here this shrine was established
He, Ame-no-Uzume and their children subsequently created the ''{{ill|Sarume clan|ja|猿女君}}'' (猿女の君).<ref name="picken-2004a">{{Cite book |last=Picken |first=Stuart D. B. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&q=Uzume&pg=PA1 |title=Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents |date=2004 |publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group |isbn=978-0-313-26432-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="roberts-2009a">{{Cite book |last=Roberts |first=Jeremy |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Uzume |title=Japanese Mythology A to Z |date=2009 |publisher=Infobase Publishing |isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="coulter-2013a">{{Cite book |last1=Coulter |first1=Charles Russell |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&dq=Ame-no-Uzume&pg=PT91 |title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities |last2=Turner |first2=Patricia |date=2013-07-04 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-135-96397-2 |language=en}}</ref> clan along with an order of female court and religious dancers. It was the origin of [[Kagura]] and [[Noh]]. Other descendants includes the Ujitoko clan from Ise province.
[[File:Sarutahiko Ōkami crop from Wittig collection.png|thumb|left|upright|Sarutahiko Ōkami; taken from a late-19th-century Japanese painting.]]
==See also== * [[Tengu]] * [[Garuda]] * [[Shirahige Jinja]]

(2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=v7Xs1NzMMHMC&pg=PA377 ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 377]; [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 3] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf|date=2013-05-17}}; retrieved 2012-8-23.</ref> That shrine is sometimes seen as the head of all Sarutahiko shrines, but other people say this one is.

He, Ame-no-Uzume and their children subsequently created the ''{{ill|Sarume clan|ja|猿女君}}'' (猿女の君).<ref name="picken-2004a">{{Cite book |last=Picken |first=Stuart D. B. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&q=Uzume&pg=PA1 |title=Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents |date=2004 |publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group |isbn=978-0-313-26432-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="roberts-2009a">{{Cite book |last=Roberts |first=Jeremy |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Uzume |title=Japanese Mythology A to Z |date=2009 |publisher=Infobase Publishing |isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="coulter-2013a">{{Cite book |last1=Coulter |first1=Charles Russell |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&dq=Ame-no-Uzume&pg=PT91 |title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities |last2=Turner |first2=Patricia |date=2013-07-04 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-135-96397-2 |language=en}}</ref> clan along with an order of female court and religious dancers. It was the origin of [[Kagura]] and [[Noh]]. Other descendants includes the Ujitoko clan from Ise province.
[[File:Sarutahiko Ōkami crop from Wittig collection.png|thumb|left|upright|Sarutahiko Ōkami; taken from a late-19th-century Japanese painting.]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Japan}}
{{Expand Japanese|猿田彦神社}}{{infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Ise Sarutahiko Shrine.jpg | caption = Shrine | deity = [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]] }} '''Sarutahiko Shrine''' (猿田彦神社) is a [[shinto shrine]] in [[Ise, Mie]].<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.sarutahikojinja.or.jp/english/ | title=Dedicated to Sarutahiko Okami, the Kami of Guidance｜みちひらきの大神 猿田彦神社 }}</ref> located close to [[Ise Grand Shrine]].<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Teeuwen |first1=Mark |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=T9rWDQAAQBAJ&dq=Sarutahiko+Shrine&pg=PT393 |title=A Social History of the Ise Shrines: Divine Capital |last2=Breen |first2=John |date=2017-02-09 |publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing |isbn=978-1-4742-7280-3 |language=en}}</ref> It is a [[beppyo shrine]] and a [[Hokora]] in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|modern system of ranked shinto shrines]].
The priests of the shrine are direct descendants of [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Dedicated to Sarutahiko Okami, the Kami of Guidance｜みちひらきの大神 猿田彦神社 |url=https://www.sarutahikojinja.or.jp/english/ |access-date=2023-10-21 |website=www.sarutahikojinja.or.jp}}</ref> who it worships.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-22 |title=Sarutahiko {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9158 |access-date=2023-12-07 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220122143817/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9158 |archive-date=2022-01-22 }}</ref>
It is linked to [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] the [[Ichinomiya]] of [[Ise Province]].<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref><ref name="mw-2012a">{{Cite web |title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120213073802/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |archive-date=2012-02-13 |access-date=2023-12-01}}</ref><ref name=":0">Picken, Stuart. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=v7Xs1NzMMHMC&pg=PA377 ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 377]; [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 3] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf|date=2013-05-17}}; retrieved 2012-8-23.</ref> That shrine is sometimes seen as the head of all Sarutahiko shrines, but other people say this one is.
==Mythical origin== {{Shinto}} [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]] ({{lang-ja|猿田毘古大神, 猿田彦大神}}) is a deity of the [[Religion in Japan|Japanese religion]] of [[Shinto]]; he is the leader of the earthly ''[[kami]],'' the [[kunitsukami]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>
In the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', he is the one who meets [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], the grandson of [[Amaterasu]], the Sun goddess, when he descends from [[Takama-ga-hara]].<ref>{{cite web|title=English|url=http://www.sarutahiko-jinjya.net/img/panf_e.pdf|publisher=尾張猿田彦神社|access-date=14 August 2012}}</ref> He is depicted as a towering man with a large beard, jeweled spear, ruddy face, and long nose. At first he is unwilling to let Ninigi pass until persuaded by [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto]], the kami of dance and the arts, whom he later marries.<ref>{{cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.tsubakishrine.org/history/index.html|publisher=Tsubaki Grand Shrine America|access-date=13 August 2012}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Ninigi|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en}}</ref> Even Sarutahiko was regarded as the "god who illuminates the sky and the earth" in Ise, and was worshiped by local believers as the '''sun god''' (personification of sun) before [[Amaterasu]].
After guiding the [[Amatsukami]] to earth, he rested at the [[Isuzu River]] and here this shrine was established
He, Ame-no-Uzume and their children subsequently created the ''{{ill|Sarume clan|ja|猿女君}}'' (猿女の君).<ref name="picken-2004a">{{Cite book |last=Picken |first=Stuart D. B. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&q=Uzume&pg=PA1 |title=Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents |date=2004 |publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group |isbn=978-0-313-26432-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="roberts-2009a">{{Cite book |last=Roberts |first=Jeremy |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Uzume |title=Japanese Mythology A to Z |date=2009 |publisher=Infobase Publishing |isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="coulter-2013a">{{Cite book |last1=Coulter |first1=Charles Russell |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&dq=Ame-no-Uzume&pg=PT91 |title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities |last2=Turner |first2=Patricia |date=2013-07-04 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-135-96397-2 |language=en}}</ref> clan along with an order of female court and religious dancers. It was the origin of [[Kagura]] and [[Noh]]. Other descendants includes the Ujitoko clan from Ise province.
[[File:Sarutahiko Ōkami crop from Wittig collection.png|thumb|left|upright|Sarutahiko Ōkami; taken from a late-19th-century Japanese painting.]]
==See also== * [[Tengu]] * [[Garuda]] * [[Shirahige Jinja]]

B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>
In the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', he is the one who meets [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], the grandson of [[Amaterasu]], the Sun goddess, when he descends from [[Takama-ga-hara]].<ref>{{cite web|title=English|url=http://www.sarutahiko-jinjya.net/img/panf_e.pdf|publisher=尾張猿田彦神社|access-date=14 August 2012}}</ref> He is depicted as a towering man with a large beard, jeweled spear, ruddy face, and long nose. At first he is unwilling to let Ninigi pass until persuaded by [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto]], the kami of dance and the arts, whom he later marries.<ref>{{cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.tsubakishrine.org/history/index.html|publisher=Tsubaki Grand Shrine America|access-date=13 August 2012}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Ninigi|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en}}</ref> Even Sarutahiko was regarded as the "god who illuminates the sky and the earth" in Ise, and was worshiped by local believers as the '''sun god''' (personification of sun) before [[Amaterasu]].
After guiding the [[Amatsukami]] to earth, he rested at the [[Isuzu River]] and here this shrine was established
He, Ame-no-Uzume and their children subsequently created the ''{{ill|Sarume clan|ja|猿女君}}'' (猿女の君).<ref name="picken-2004a">{{Cite book |last=Picken |first=Stuart D. B. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&q=Uzume&pg=PA1 |title=Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents |date=2004 |publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group |isbn=978-0-313-26432-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="roberts-2009a">{{Cite book |last=Roberts |first=Jeremy |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Uzume |title=Japanese Mythology A to Z |date=2009 |publisher=Infobase Publishing |isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="coulter-2013a">{{Cite book |last1=Coulter |first1=Charles Russell |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&dq=Ame-no-Uzume&pg=PT91 |title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities |last2=Turner |first2=Patricia |date=2013-07-04 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-135-96397-2 |language=en}}</ref> clan along with an order of female court and religious dancers. It was the origin of [[Kagura]] and [[Noh]]. Other descendants includes the Ujitoko clan from Ise province.
[[File:Sarutahiko Ōkami crop from Wittig collection.png|thumb|left|upright|Sarutahiko Ōkami; taken from a late-19th-century Japanese painting.]]
==See also== * [[Tengu]] * [[Garuda]] * [[Shirahige Jinja]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Japan}}
{{Expand Japanese|猿田彦神社}}{{infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Ise Sarutahiko Shrine.jpg | caption = Shrine | deity = [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]] }} '''Sarutahiko Shrine''' (猿田彦神社) is a [[shinto shrine]] in [[Ise, Mie]].<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.sarutahikojinja.or.jp/english/ | title=Dedicated to Sarutahiko Okami, the Kami of Guidance｜みちひらきの大神 猿田彦神社 }}</ref> located close to [[Ise Grand Shrine]].<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Teeuwen |first1=Mark |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=T9rWDQAAQBAJ&dq=Sarutahiko+Shrine&pg=PT393 |title=A Social History of the Ise Shrines: Divine Capital |last2=Breen |first2=John |date=2017-02-09 |publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing |isbn=978-1-4742-7280-3 |language=en}}</ref> It is a [[beppyo shrine]] and a [[Hokora]] in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|modern system of ranked shinto shrines]].
The priests of the shrine are direct descendants of [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Dedicated to Sarutahiko Okami, the Kami of Guidance｜みちひらきの大神 猿田彦神社 |url=https://www.sarutahikojinja.or.jp/english/ |access-date=2023-10-21 |website=www.sarutahikojinja.or.jp}}</ref> who it worships.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-22 |title=Sarutahiko {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9158 |access-date=2023-12-07 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220122143817/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9158 |archive-date=2022-01-22 }}</ref>
It is linked to [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] the [[Ichinomiya]] of [[Ise Province]].<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref><ref name="mw-2012a">{{Cite web |title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120213073802/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |archive-date=2012-02-13 |access-date=2023-12-01}}</ref><ref name=":0">Picken, Stuart. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=v7Xs1NzMMHMC&pg=PA377 ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 377]; [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 3] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf|date=2013-05-17}}; retrieved 2012-8-23.</ref> That shrine is sometimes seen as the head of all Sarutahiko shrines, but other people say this one is.
==Mythical origin== {{Shinto}} [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]] ({{lang-ja|猿田毘古大神, 猿田彦大神}}) is a deity of the [[Religion in Japan|Japanese religion]] of [[Shinto]]; he is the leader of the earthly ''[[kami]],'' the [[kunitsukami]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>
In the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', he is the one who meets [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], the grandson of [[Amaterasu]], the Sun goddess, when he descends from [[Takama-ga-hara]].<ref>{{cite web|title=English|url=http://www.sarutahiko-jinjya.net/img/panf_e.pdf|publisher=尾張猿田彦神社|access-date=14 August 2012}}</ref> He is depicted as a towering man with a large beard, jeweled spear, ruddy face, and long nose. At first he is unwilling to let Ninigi pass until persuaded by [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto]], the kami of dance and the arts, whom he later marries.<ref>{{cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.tsubakishrine.org/history/index.html|publisher=Tsubaki Grand Shrine America|access-date=13 August 2012}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Ninigi|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en}}</ref> Even Sarutahiko was regarded as the "god who illuminates the sky and the earth" in Ise, and was worshiped by local believers as the '''sun god''' (personification of sun) before [[Amaterasu]].
After guiding the [[Amatsukami]] to earth, he rested at the [[Isuzu River]] and here this shrine was established
He, Ame-no-Uzume and their children subsequently created the ''{{ill|Sarume clan|ja|猿女君}}'' (猿女の君).<ref name="picken-2004a">{{Cite book |last=Picken |first=Stuart D. B. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&q=Uzume&pg=PA1 |title=Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents |date=2004 |publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group |isbn=978-0-313-26432-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="roberts-2009a">{{Cite book |last=Roberts |first=Jeremy |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Uzume |title=Japanese Mythology A to Z |date=2009 |publisher=Infobase Publishing |isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="coulter-2013a">{{Cite book |last1=Coulter |first1=Charles Russell |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&dq=Ame-no-Uzume&pg=PT91 |title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities |last2=Turner |first2=Patricia |date=2013-07-04 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-135-96397-2 |language=en}}</ref> clan along with an order of female court and religious dancers. It was the origin of [[Kagura]] and [[Noh]]. Other descendants includes the Ujitoko clan from Ise province.
[[File:Sarutahiko Ōkami crop from Wittig collection.png|thumb|left|upright|Sarutahiko Ōkami; taken from a late-19th-century Japanese painting.]]

(2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=v7Xs1NzMMHMC&pg=PA377 ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 377]; [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 3] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf|date=2013-05-17}}; retrieved 2012-8-23.</ref> That shrine is sometimes seen as the head of all Sarutahiko shrines, but other people say this one is.

B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>

{{Expand Japanese|猿田彦神社}}{{infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Ise Sarutahiko Shrine.jpg | caption = Shrine | deity = [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]] }} '''Sarutahiko Shrine''' (猿田彦神社) is a [[shinto shrine]] in [[Ise, Mie]].<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.sarutahikojinja.or.jp/english/ | title=Dedicated to Sarutahiko Okami, the Kami of Guidance｜みちひらきの大神 猿田彦神社 }}</ref> located close to [[Ise Grand Shrine]].<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Teeuwen |first1=Mark |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=T9rWDQAAQBAJ&dq=Sarutahiko+Shrine&pg=PT393 |title=A Social History of the Ise Shrines: Divine Capital |last2=Breen |first2=John |date=2017-02-09 |publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing |isbn=978-1-4742-7280-3 |language=en}}</ref> It is a [[beppyo shrine]] and a [[Hokora]] in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|modern system of ranked shinto shrines]].

It is linked to [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] the [[Ichinomiya]] of [[Ise Province]].<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref><ref name="mw-2012a">{{Cite web |title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120213073802/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |archive-date=2012-02-13 |access-date=2023-12-01}}</ref><ref name=":0">Picken, Stuart. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=v7Xs1NzMMHMC&pg=PA377 ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 377]; [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 3] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf|date=2013-05-17}}; retrieved 2012-8-23.</ref> That shrine is sometimes seen as the head of all Sarutahiko shrines, but other people say this one is.
==Mythical origin== {{Shinto}} [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]] ({{lang-ja|猿田毘古大神, 猿田彦大神}}) is a deity of the [[Religion in Japan|Japanese religion]] of [[Shinto]]; he is the leader of the earthly ''[[kami]],'' the [[kunitsukami]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>
In the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', he is the one who meets [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], the grandson of [[Amaterasu]], the Sun goddess, when he descends from [[Takama-ga-hara]].<ref>{{cite web|title=English|url=http://www.sarutahiko-jinjya.net/img/panf_e.pdf|publisher=尾張猿田彦神社|access-date=14 August 2012}}</ref> He is depicted as a towering man with a large beard, jeweled spear, ruddy face, and long nose. At first he is unwilling to let Ninigi pass until persuaded by [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto]], the kami of dance and the arts, whom he later marries.<ref>{{cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.tsubakishrine.org/history/index.html|publisher=Tsubaki Grand Shrine America|access-date=13 August 2012}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Ninigi|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en}}</ref> Even Sarutahiko was regarded as the "god who illuminates the sky and the earth" in Ise, and was worshiped by local believers as the '''sun god''' (personification of sun) before [[Amaterasu]].
After guiding the [[Amatsukami]] to earth, he rested at the [[Isuzu River]] and here this shrine was established
He, Ame-no-Uzume and their children subsequently created the ''{{ill|Sarume clan|ja|猿女君}}'' (猿女の君).<ref name="picken-2004a">{{Cite book |last=Picken |first=Stuart D. B. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&q=Uzume&pg=PA1 |title=Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents |date=2004 |publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group |isbn=978-0-313-26432-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="roberts-2009a">{{Cite book |last=Roberts |first=Jeremy |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Uzume |title=Japanese Mythology A to Z |date=2009 |publisher=Infobase Publishing |isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="coulter-2013a">{{Cite book |last1=Coulter |first1=Charles Russell |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&dq=Ame-no-Uzume&pg=PT91 |title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities |last2=Turner |first2=Patricia |date=2013-07-04 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-135-96397-2 |language=en}}</ref> clan along with an order of female court and religious dancers. It was the origin of [[Kagura]] and [[Noh]]. Other descendants includes the Ujitoko clan from Ise province.
[[File:Sarutahiko Ōkami crop from Wittig collection.png|thumb|left|upright|Sarutahiko Ōkami; taken from a late-19th-century Japanese painting.]]
==See also== * [[Tengu]] * [[Garuda]] * [[Shirahige Jinja]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Japan}}
{{Expand Japanese|猿田彦神社}}{{infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Ise Sarutahiko Shrine.jpg | caption = Shrine | deity = [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]] }} '''Sarutahiko Shrine''' (猿田彦神社) is a [[shinto shrine]] in [[Ise, Mie]].<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.sarutahikojinja.or.jp/english/ | title=Dedicated to Sarutahiko Okami, the Kami of Guidance｜みちひらきの大神 猿田彦神社 }}</ref> located close to [[Ise Grand Shrine]].<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Teeuwen |first1=Mark |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=T9rWDQAAQBAJ&dq=Sarutahiko+Shrine&pg=PT393 |title=A Social History of the Ise Shrines: Divine Capital |last2=Breen |first2=John |date=2017-02-09 |publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing |isbn=978-1-4742-7280-3 |language=en}}</ref> It is a [[beppyo shrine]] and a [[Hokora]] in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|modern system of ranked shinto shrines]].
The priests of the shrine are direct descendants of [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Dedicated to Sarutahiko Okami, the Kami of Guidance｜みちひらきの大神 猿田彦神社 |url=https://www.sarutahikojinja.or.jp/english/ |access-date=2023-10-21 |website=www.sarutahikojinja.or.jp}}</ref> who it worships.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-22 |title=Sarutahiko {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9158 |access-date=2023-12-07 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220122143817/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9158 |archive-date=2022-01-22 }}</ref>
It is linked to [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] the [[Ichinomiya]] of [[Ise Province]].<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref><ref name="mw-2012a">{{Cite web |title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120213073802/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |archive-date=2012-02-13 |access-date=2023-12-01}}</ref><ref name=":0">Picken, Stuart. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>

At first he is unwilling to let Ninigi pass until persuaded by [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto]], the kami of dance and the arts, whom he later marries.<ref>{{cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.tsubakishrine.org/history/index.html|publisher=Tsubaki Grand Shrine America|access-date=13 August 2012}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Ninigi|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en}}</ref> Even Sarutahiko was regarded as the "god who illuminates the sky and the earth" in Ise, and was worshiped by local believers as the '''sun god''' (personification of sun) before [[Amaterasu]].

B. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&q=Uzume&pg=PA1 |title=Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents |date=2004 |publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group |isbn=978-0-313-26432-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="roberts-2009a">{{Cite book |last=Roberts |first=Jeremy |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Uzume |title=Japanese Mythology A to Z |date=2009 |publisher=Infobase Publishing |isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="coulter-2013a">{{Cite book |last1=Coulter |first1=Charles Russell |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&dq=Ame-no-Uzume&pg=PT91 |title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities |last2=Turner |first2=Patricia |date=2013-07-04 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-135-96397-2 |language=en}}</ref> clan along with an order of female court and religious dancers. revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Japan}}
{{Expand Japanese|猿田彦神社}}{{infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Ise Sarutahiko Shrine.jpg | caption = Shrine | deity = [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]] }} '''Sarutahiko Shrine''' (猿田彦神社) is a [[shinto shrine]] in [[Ise, Mie]].<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.sarutahikojinja.or.jp/english/ | title=Dedicated to Sarutahiko Okami, the Kami of Guidance｜みちひらきの大神 猿田彦神社 }}</ref> located close to [[Ise Grand Shrine]].<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Teeuwen |first1=Mark |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=T9rWDQAAQBAJ&dq=Sarutahiko+Shrine&pg=PT393 |title=A Social History of the Ise Shrines: Divine Capital |last2=Breen |first2=John |date=2017-02-09 |publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing |isbn=978-1-4742-7280-3 |language=en}}</ref> It is a [[beppyo shrine]] and a [[Hokora]] in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|modern system of ranked shinto shrines]].
The priests of the shrine are direct descendants of [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Dedicated to Sarutahiko Okami, the Kami of Guidance｜みちひらきの大神 猿田彦神社 |url=https://www.sarutahikojinja.or.jp/english/ |access-date=2023-10-21 |website=www.sarutahikojinja.or.jp}}</ref> who it worships.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-22 |title=Sarutahiko {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9158 |access-date=2023-12-07 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220122143817/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9158 |archive-date=2022-01-22 }}</ref>
It is linked to [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] the [[Ichinomiya]] of [[Ise Province]].<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref><ref name="mw-2012a">{{Cite web |title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120213073802/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |archive-date=2012-02-13 |access-date=2023-12-01}}</ref><ref name=":0">Picken, Stuart. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=v7Xs1NzMMHMC&pg=PA377 ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 377]; [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 3] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf|date=2013-05-17}}; retrieved 2012-8-23.</ref> That shrine is sometimes seen as the head of all Sarutahiko shrines, but other people say this one is.
==Mythical origin== {{Shinto}} [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]] ({{lang-ja|猿田毘古大神, 猿田彦大神}}) is a deity of the [[Religion in Japan|Japanese religion]] of [[Shinto]]; he is the leader of the earthly ''[[kami]],'' the [[kunitsukami]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>
In the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', he is the one who meets [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], the grandson of [[Amaterasu]], the Sun goddess, when he descends from [[Takama-ga-hara]].<ref>{{cite web|title=English|url=http://www.sarutahiko-jinjya.net/img/panf_e.pdf|publisher=尾張猿田彦神社|access-date=14 August 2012}}</ref> He is depicted as a towering man with a large beard, jeweled spear, ruddy face, and long nose. At first he is unwilling to let Ninigi pass until persuaded by [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto]], the kami of dance and the arts, whom he later marries.<ref>{{cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.tsubakishrine.org/history/index.html|publisher=Tsubaki Grand Shrine America|access-date=13 August 2012}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Ninigi|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en}}</ref> Even Sarutahiko was regarded as the "god who illuminates the sky and the earth" in Ise, and was worshiped by local believers as the '''sun god''' (personification of sun) before [[Amaterasu]].
After guiding the [[Amatsukami]] to earth, he rested at the [[Isuzu River]] and here this shrine was established
He, Ame-no-Uzume and their children subsequently created the ''{{ill|Sarume clan|ja|猿女君}}'' (猿女の君).<ref name="picken-2004a">{{Cite book |last=Picken |first=Stuart D. B. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&q=Uzume&pg=PA1 |title=Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents |date=2004 |publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group |isbn=978-0-313-26432-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="roberts-2009a">{{Cite book |last=Roberts |first=Jeremy |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Uzume |title=Japanese Mythology A to Z |date=2009 |publisher=Infobase Publishing |isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="coulter-2013a">{{Cite book |last1=Coulter |first1=Charles Russell |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&dq=Ame-no-Uzume&pg=PT91 |title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities |last2=Turner |first2=Patricia |date=2013-07-04 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-135-96397-2 |language=en}}</ref> clan along with an order of female court and religious dancers. It was the origin of [[Kagura]] and [[Noh]]. Other descendants includes the Ujitoko clan from Ise province.
[[File:Sarutahiko Ōkami crop from Wittig collection.png|thumb|left|upright|Sarutahiko Ōkami; taken from a late-19th-century Japanese painting.]]
==See also== * [[Tengu]] * [[Garuda]] * [[Shirahige Jinja]]

(2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=v7Xs1NzMMHMC&pg=PA377 ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 377]; [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 3] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf|date=2013-05-17}}; retrieved 2012-8-23.</ref> That shrine is sometimes seen as the head of all Sarutahiko shrines, but other people say this one is.

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Japan}}
{{Expand Japanese|猿田彦神社}}{{infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Ise Sarutahiko Shrine.jpg | caption = Shrine | deity = [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]] }} '''Sarutahiko Shrine''' (猿田彦神社) is a [[shinto shrine]] in [[Ise, Mie]].<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.sarutahikojinja.or.jp/english/ | title=Dedicated to Sarutahiko Okami, the Kami of Guidance｜みちひらきの大神 猿田彦神社 }}</ref> located close to [[Ise Grand Shrine]].<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Teeuwen |first1=Mark |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=T9rWDQAAQBAJ&dq=Sarutahiko+Shrine&pg=PT393 |title=A Social History of the Ise Shrines: Divine Capital |last2=Breen |first2=John |date=2017-02-09 |publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing |isbn=978-1-4742-7280-3 |language=en}}</ref> It is a [[beppyo shrine]] and a [[Hokora]] in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|modern system of ranked shinto shrines]].
The priests of the shrine are direct descendants of [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Dedicated to Sarutahiko Okami, the Kami of Guidance｜みちひらきの大神 猿田彦神社 |url=https://www.sarutahikojinja.or.jp/english/ |access-date=2023-10-21 |website=www.sarutahikojinja.or.jp}}</ref> who it worships.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-22 |title=Sarutahiko {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9158 |access-date=2023-12-07 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220122143817/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9158 |archive-date=2022-01-22 }}</ref>
It is linked to [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] the [[Ichinomiya]] of [[Ise Province]].<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref><ref name="mw-2012a">{{Cite web |title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120213073802/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |archive-date=2012-02-13 |access-date=2023-12-01}}</ref><ref name=":0">Picken, Stuart. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=v7Xs1NzMMHMC&pg=PA377 ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 377]; [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 3] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf|date=2013-05-17}}; retrieved 2012-8-23.</ref> That shrine is sometimes seen as the head of all Sarutahiko shrines, but other people say this one is.
==Mythical origin== {{Shinto}} [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]] ({{lang-ja|猿田毘古大神, 猿田彦大神}}) is a deity of the [[Religion in Japan|Japanese religion]] of [[Shinto]]; he is the leader of the earthly ''[[kami]],'' the [[kunitsukami]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>
In the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', he is the one who meets [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], the grandson of [[Amaterasu]], the Sun goddess, when he descends from [[Takama-ga-hara]].<ref>{{cite web|title=English|url=http://www.sarutahiko-jinjya.net/img/panf_e.pdf|publisher=尾張猿田彦神社|access-date=14 August 2012}}</ref> He is depicted as a towering man with a large beard, jeweled spear, ruddy face, and long nose. At first he is unwilling to let Ninigi pass until persuaded by [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto]], the kami of dance and the arts, whom he later marries.<ref>{{cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.tsubakishrine.org/history/index.html|publisher=Tsubaki Grand Shrine America|access-date=13 August 2012}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Ninigi|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en}}</ref> Even Sarutahiko was regarded as the "god who illuminates the sky and the earth" in Ise, and was worshiped by local believers as the '''sun god''' (personification of sun) before [[Amaterasu]].
After guiding the [[Amatsukami]] to earth, he rested at the [[Isuzu River]] and here this shrine was established
He, Ame-no-Uzume and their children subsequently created the ''{{ill|Sarume clan|ja|猿女君}}'' (猿女の君).<ref name="picken-2004a">{{Cite book |last=Picken |first=Stuart D. B. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&q=Uzume&pg=PA1 |title=Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents |date=2004 |publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group |isbn=978-0-313-26432-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="roberts-2009a">{{Cite book |last=Roberts |first=Jeremy |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Uzume |title=Japanese Mythology A to Z |date=2009 |publisher=Infobase Publishing |isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="coulter-2013a">{{Cite book |last1=Coulter |first1=Charles Russell |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&dq=Ame-no-Uzume&pg=PT91 |title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities |last2=Turner |first2=Patricia |date=2013-07-04 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-135-96397-2 |language=en}}</ref> clan along with an order of female court and religious dancers. It was the origin of [[Kagura]] and [[Noh]]. Other descendants includes the Ujitoko clan from Ise province.
[[File:Sarutahiko Ōkami crop from Wittig collection.png|thumb|left|upright|Sarutahiko Ōkami; taken from a late-19th-century Japanese painting.]]

[https://books.google.com/books?id=v7Xs1NzMMHMC&pg=PA377 ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 377]; [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 3] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf|date=2013-05-17}}; retrieved 2012-8-23.</ref> That shrine is sometimes seen as the head of all Sarutahiko shrines, but other people say this one is.

He, Ame-no-Uzume and their children subsequently created the ''{{ill|Sarume clan|ja|猿女君}}'' (猿女の君).<ref name="picken-2004a">{{Cite book |last=Picken |first=Stuart D. B. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&q=Uzume&pg=PA1 |title=Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents |date=2004 |publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group |isbn=978-0-313-26432-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="roberts-2009a">{{Cite book |last=Roberts |first=Jeremy |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Uzume |title=Japanese Mythology A to Z |date=2009 |publisher=Infobase Publishing |isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="coulter-2013a">{{Cite book |last1=Coulter |first1=Charles Russell |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&dq=Ame-no-Uzume&pg=PT91 |title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities |last2=Turner |first2=Patricia |date=2013-07-04 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-135-96397-2 |language=en}}</ref> clan along with an order of female court and religious dancers. It was the origin of [[Kagura]] and [[Noh]]. Other descendants includes the Ujitoko clan from Ise province.
[[File:Sarutahiko Ōkami crop from Wittig collection.png|thumb|left|upright|Sarutahiko Ōkami; taken from a late-19th-century Japanese painting.]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Japan}}
{{Expand Japanese|猿田彦神社}}{{infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Ise Sarutahiko Shrine.jpg | caption = Shrine | deity = [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]] }} '''Sarutahiko Shrine''' (猿田彦神社) is a [[shinto shrine]] in [[Ise, Mie]].<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.sarutahikojinja.or.jp/english/ | title=Dedicated to Sarutahiko Okami, the Kami of Guidance｜みちひらきの大神 猿田彦神社 }}</ref> located close to [[Ise Grand Shrine]].<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Teeuwen |first1=Mark |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=T9rWDQAAQBAJ&dq=Sarutahiko+Shrine&pg=PT393 |title=A Social History of the Ise Shrines: Divine Capital |last2=Breen |first2=John |date=2017-02-09 |publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing |isbn=978-1-4742-7280-3 |language=en}}</ref> It is a [[beppyo shrine]] and a [[Hokora]] in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|modern system of ranked shinto shrines]].
The priests of the shrine are direct descendants of [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Dedicated to Sarutahiko Okami, the Kami of Guidance｜みちひらきの大神 猿田彦神社 |url=https://www.sarutahikojinja.or.jp/english/ |access-date=2023-10-21 |website=www.sarutahikojinja.or.jp}}</ref> who it worships.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-22 |title=Sarutahiko {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9158 |access-date=2023-12-07 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220122143817/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9158 |archive-date=2022-01-22 }}</ref>
It is linked to [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] the [[Ichinomiya]] of [[Ise Province]].<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref><ref name="mw-2012a">{{Cite web |title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120213073802/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |archive-date=2012-02-13 |access-date=2023-12-01}}</ref><ref name=":0">Picken, Stuart. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=v7Xs1NzMMHMC&pg=PA377 ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 377]; [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 3] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf|date=2013-05-17}}; retrieved 2012-8-23.</ref> That shrine is sometimes seen as the head of all Sarutahiko shrines, but other people say this one is.
==Mythical origin== {{Shinto}} [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]] ({{lang-ja|猿田毘古大神, 猿田彦大神}}) is a deity of the [[Religion in Japan|Japanese religion]] of [[Shinto]]; he is the leader of the earthly ''[[kami]],'' the [[kunitsukami]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>
In the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', he is the one who meets [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], the grandson of [[Amaterasu]], the Sun goddess, when he descends from [[Takama-ga-hara]].<ref>{{cite web|title=English|url=http://www.sarutahiko-jinjya.net/img/panf_e.pdf|publisher=尾張猿田彦神社|access-date=14 August 2012}}</ref> He is depicted as a towering man with a large beard, jeweled spear, ruddy face, and long nose. At first he is unwilling to let Ninigi pass until persuaded by [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto]], the kami of dance and the arts, whom he later marries.<ref>{{cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.tsubakishrine.org/history/index.html|publisher=Tsubaki Grand Shrine America|access-date=13 August 2012}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Ninigi|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en}}</ref> Even Sarutahiko was regarded as the "god who illuminates the sky and the earth" in Ise, and was worshiped by local believers as the '''sun god''' (personification of sun) before [[Amaterasu]].
After guiding the [[Amatsukami]] to earth, he rested at the [[Isuzu River]] and here this shrine was established
He, Ame-no-Uzume and their children subsequently created the ''{{ill|Sarume clan|ja|猿女君}}'' (猿女の君).<ref name="picken-2004a">{{Cite book |last=Picken |first=Stuart D. B. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&q=Uzume&pg=PA1 |title=Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents |date=2004 |publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group |isbn=978-0-313-26432-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="roberts-2009a">{{Cite book |last=Roberts |first=Jeremy |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Uzume |title=Japanese Mythology A to Z |date=2009 |publisher=Infobase Publishing |isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="coulter-2013a">{{Cite book |last1=Coulter |first1=Charles Russell |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&dq=Ame-no-Uzume&pg=PT91 |title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities |last2=Turner |first2=Patricia |date=2013-07-04 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-135-96397-2 |language=en}}</ref> clan along with an order of female court and religious dancers. It was the origin of [[Kagura]] and [[Noh]]. Other descendants includes the Ujitoko clan from Ise province.
[[File:Sarutahiko Ōkami crop from Wittig collection.png|thumb|left|upright|Sarutahiko Ōkami; taken from a late-19th-century Japanese painting.]]

(2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=v7Xs1NzMMHMC&pg=PA377 ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 377]; [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 3] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf|date=2013-05-17}}; retrieved 2012-8-23.</ref> That shrine is sometimes seen as the head of all Sarutahiko shrines, but other people say this one is.
==Mythical origin== {{Shinto}} [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]] ({{lang-ja|猿田毘古大神, 猿田彦大神}}) is a deity of the [[Religion in Japan|Japanese religion]] of [[Shinto]]; he is the leader of the earthly ''[[kami]],'' the [[kunitsukami]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>
In the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', he is the one who meets [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], the grandson of [[Amaterasu]], the Sun goddess, when he descends from [[Takama-ga-hara]].<ref>{{cite web|title=English|url=http://www.sarutahiko-jinjya.net/img/panf_e.pdf|publisher=尾張猿田彦神社|access-date=14 August 2012}}</ref> He is depicted as a towering man with a large beard, jeweled spear, ruddy face, and long nose. At first he is unwilling to let Ninigi pass until persuaded by [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto]], the kami of dance and the arts, whom he later marries.<ref>{{cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.tsubakishrine.org/history/index.html|publisher=Tsubaki Grand Shrine America|access-date=13 August 2012}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Ninigi|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en}}</ref> Even Sarutahiko was regarded as the "god who illuminates the sky and the earth" in Ise, and was worshiped by local believers as the '''sun god''' (personification of sun) before [[Amaterasu]].
After guiding the [[Amatsukami]] to earth, he rested at the [[Isuzu River]] and here this shrine was established
He, Ame-no-Uzume and their children subsequently created the ''{{ill|Sarume clan|ja|猿女君}}'' (猿女の君).<ref name="picken-2004a">{{Cite book |last=Picken |first=Stuart D. B. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&q=Uzume&pg=PA1 |title=Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents |date=2004 |publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group |isbn=978-0-313-26432-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="roberts-2009a">{{Cite book |last=Roberts |first=Jeremy |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Uzume |title=Japanese Mythology A to Z |date=2009 |publisher=Infobase Publishing |isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="coulter-2013a">{{Cite book |last1=Coulter |first1=Charles Russell |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&dq=Ame-no-Uzume&pg=PT91 |title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities |last2=Turner |first2=Patricia |date=2013-07-04 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-135-96397-2 |language=en}}</ref> clan along with an order of female court and religious dancers. It was the origin of [[Kagura]] and [[Noh]]. Other descendants includes the Ujitoko clan from Ise province.
[[File:Sarutahiko Ōkami crop from Wittig collection.png|thumb|left|upright|Sarutahiko Ōkami; taken from a late-19th-century Japanese painting.]]

It is linked to [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] the [[Ichinomiya]] of [[Ise Province]].<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref><ref name="mw-2012a">{{Cite web |title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120213073802/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |archive-date=2012-02-13 |access-date=2023-12-01}}</ref><ref name=":0">Picken, Stuart. {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Japan}}
{{Expand Japanese|猿田彦神社}}{{infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Ise Sarutahiko Shrine.jpg | caption = Shrine | deity = [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]] }} '''Sarutahiko Shrine''' (猿田彦神社) is a [[shinto shrine]] in [[Ise, Mie]].<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.sarutahikojinja.or.jp/english/ | title=Dedicated to Sarutahiko Okami, the Kami of Guidance｜みちひらきの大神 猿田彦神社 }}</ref> located close to [[Ise Grand Shrine]].<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Teeuwen |first1=Mark |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=T9rWDQAAQBAJ&dq=Sarutahiko+Shrine&pg=PT393 |title=A Social History of the Ise Shrines: Divine Capital |last2=Breen |first2=John |date=2017-02-09 |publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing |isbn=978-1-4742-7280-3 |language=en}}</ref> It is a [[beppyo shrine]] and a [[Hokora]] in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|modern system of ranked shinto shrines]].
The priests of the shrine are direct descendants of [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Dedicated to Sarutahiko Okami, the Kami of Guidance｜みちひらきの大神 猿田彦神社 |url=https://www.sarutahikojinja.or.jp/english/ |access-date=2023-10-21 |website=www.sarutahikojinja.or.jp}}</ref> who it worships.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-22 |title=Sarutahiko {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9158 |access-date=2023-12-07 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220122143817/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9158 |archive-date=2022-01-22 }}</ref>
It is linked to [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] the [[Ichinomiya]] of [[Ise Province]].<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref><ref name="mw-2012a">{{Cite web |title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120213073802/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |archive-date=2012-02-13 |access-date=2023-12-01}}</ref><ref name=":0">Picken, Stuart. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=v7Xs1NzMMHMC&pg=PA377 ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 377]; [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 3] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf|date=2013-05-17}}; retrieved 2012-8-23.</ref> That shrine is sometimes seen as the head of all Sarutahiko shrines, but other people say this one is.
==Mythical origin== {{Shinto}} [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]] ({{lang-ja|猿田毘古大神, 猿田彦大神}}) is a deity of the [[Religion in Japan|Japanese religion]] of [[Shinto]]; he is the leader of the earthly ''[[kami]],'' the [[kunitsukami]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>
In the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', he is the one who meets [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], the grandson of [[Amaterasu]], the Sun goddess, when he descends from [[Takama-ga-hara]].<ref>{{cite web|title=English|url=http://www.sarutahiko-jinjya.net/img/panf_e.pdf|publisher=尾張猿田彦神社|access-date=14 August 2012}}</ref> He is depicted as a towering man with a large beard, jeweled spear, ruddy face, and long nose. At first he is unwilling to let Ninigi pass until persuaded by [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto]], the kami of dance and the arts, whom he later marries.<ref>{{cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.tsubakishrine.org/history/index.html|publisher=Tsubaki Grand Shrine America|access-date=13 August 2012}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Ninigi|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en}}</ref> Even Sarutahiko was regarded as the "god who illuminates the sky and the earth" in Ise, and was worshiped by local believers as the '''sun god''' (personification of sun) before [[Amaterasu]].
After guiding the [[Amatsukami]] to earth, he rested at the [[Isuzu River]] and here this shrine was established
He, Ame-no-Uzume and their children subsequently created the ''{{ill|Sarume clan|ja|猿女君}}'' (猿女の君).<ref name="picken-2004a">{{Cite book |last=Picken |first=Stuart D. B. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&q=Uzume&pg=PA1 |title=Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents |date=2004 |publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group |isbn=978-0-313-26432-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="roberts-2009a">{{Cite book |last=Roberts |first=Jeremy |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Uzume |title=Japanese Mythology A to Z |date=2009 |publisher=Infobase Publishing |isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="coulter-2013a">{{Cite book |last1=Coulter |first1=Charles Russell |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&dq=Ame-no-Uzume&pg=PT91 |title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities |last2=Turner |first2=Patricia |date=2013-07-04 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-135-96397-2 |language=en}}</ref> clan along with an order of female court and religious dancers. It was the origin of [[Kagura]] and [[Noh]]. Other descendants includes the Ujitoko clan from Ise province.
[[File:Sarutahiko Ōkami crop from Wittig collection.png|thumb|left|upright|Sarutahiko Ōkami; taken from a late-19th-century Japanese painting.]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Japan}}
{{Expand Japanese|猿田彦神社}}{{infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Ise Sarutahiko Shrine.jpg | caption = Shrine | deity = [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]] }} '''Sarutahiko Shrine''' (猿田彦神社) is a [[shinto shrine]] in [[Ise, Mie]].<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.sarutahikojinja.or.jp/english/ | title=Dedicated to Sarutahiko Okami, the Kami of Guidance｜みちひらきの大神 猿田彦神社 }}</ref> located close to [[Ise Grand Shrine]].<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Teeuwen |first1=Mark |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=T9rWDQAAQBAJ&dq=Sarutahiko+Shrine&pg=PT393 |title=A Social History of the Ise Shrines: Divine Capital |last2=Breen |first2=John |date=2017-02-09 |publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing |isbn=978-1-4742-7280-3 |language=en}}</ref> It is a [[beppyo shrine]] and a [[Hokora]] in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|modern system of ranked shinto shrines]].
The priests of the shrine are direct descendants of [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Dedicated to Sarutahiko Okami, the Kami of Guidance｜みちひらきの大神 猿田彦神社 |url=https://www.sarutahikojinja.or.jp/english/ |access-date=2023-10-21 |website=www.sarutahikojinja.or.jp}}</ref> who it worships.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-22 |title=Sarutahiko {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9158 |access-date=2023-12-07 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220122143817/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9158 |archive-date=2022-01-22 }}</ref>
It is linked to [[Tsubaki Grand Shrine]] the [[Ichinomiya]] of [[Ise Province]].<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref><ref name="mw-2012a">{{Cite web |title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120213073802/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |archive-date=2012-02-13 |access-date=2023-12-01}}</ref><ref name=":0">Picken, Stuart. (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=v7Xs1NzMMHMC&pg=PA377 ''Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents,'' p. 377]; [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 3] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf|date=2013-05-17}}; retrieved 2012-8-23.</ref> That shrine is sometimes seen as the head of all Sarutahiko shrines, but other people say this one is.
==Mythical origin== {{Shinto}} [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]] ({{lang-ja|猿田毘古大神, 猿田彦大神}}) is a deity of the [[Religion in Japan|Japanese religion]] of [[Shinto]]; he is the leader of the earthly ''[[kami]],'' the [[kunitsukami]]<ref>{{Cite book|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>
In the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', he is the one who meets [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], the grandson of [[Amaterasu]], the Sun goddess, when he descends from [[Takama-ga-hara]].<ref>{{cite web|title=English|url=http://www.sarutahiko-jinjya.net/img/panf_e.pdf|publisher=尾張猿田彦神社|access-date=14 August 2012}}</ref> He is depicted as a towering man with a large beard, jeweled spear, ruddy face, and long nose. At first he is unwilling to let Ninigi pass until persuaded by [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto]], the kami of dance and the arts, whom he later marries.<ref>{{cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.tsubakishrine.org/history/index.html|publisher=Tsubaki Grand Shrine America|access-date=13 August 2012}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Ninigi|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en}}</ref> Even Sarutahiko was regarded as the "god who illuminates the sky and the earth" in Ise, and was worshiped by local believers as the '''sun god''' (personification of sun) before [[Amaterasu]].
After guiding the [[Amatsukami]] to earth, he rested at the [[Isuzu River]] and here this shrine was established
He, Ame-no-Uzume and their children subsequently created the ''{{ill|Sarume clan|ja|猿女君}}'' (猿女の君).<ref name="picken-2004a">{{Cite book |last=Picken |first=Stuart D. B. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&q=Uzume&pg=PA1 |title=Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents |date=2004 |publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group |isbn=978-0-313-26432-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="roberts-2009a">{{Cite book |last=Roberts |first=Jeremy |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Uzume |title=Japanese Mythology A to Z |date=2009 |publisher=Infobase Publishing |isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="coulter-2013a">{{Cite book |last1=Coulter |first1=Charles Russell |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&dq=Ame-no-Uzume&pg=PT91 |title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities |last2=Turner |first2=Patricia |date=2013-07-04 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-135-96397-2 |language=en}}</ref> clan along with an order of female court and religious dancers. It was the origin of [[Kagura]] and [[Noh]]. Other descendants includes the Ujitoko clan from Ise province.
[[File:Sarutahiko Ōkami crop from Wittig collection.png|thumb|left|upright|Sarutahiko Ōkami; taken from a late-19th-century Japanese painting.]]

B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlG3F6gSm9UC&dq=Sarutahiko+earth+kami&pg=PA76|title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto|date=2010-12-28|publisher=Scarecrow Press|isbn=978-0-8108-7372-8|pages=76|language=en}}</ref>

At first he is unwilling to let Ninigi pass until persuaded by [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto]], the kami of dance and the arts, whom he later marries.<ref>{{cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.tsubakishrine.org/history/index.html|publisher=Tsubaki Grand Shrine America|access-date=13 August 2012}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Ninigi|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en}}</ref> Even Sarutahiko was regarded as the "god who illuminates the sky and the earth" in Ise, and was worshiped by local believers as the '''sun god''' (personification of sun) before [[Amaterasu]].
After guiding the [[Amatsukami]] to earth, he rested at the [[Isuzu River]] and here this shrine was established
He, Ame-no-Uzume and their children subsequently created the ''{{ill|Sarume clan|ja|猿女君}}'' (猿女の君).<ref name="picken-2004a">{{Cite book |last=Picken |first=Stuart D. B. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&q=Uzume&pg=PA1 |title=Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents |date=2004 |publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group |isbn=978-0-313-26432-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="roberts-2009a">{{Cite book |last=Roberts |first=Jeremy |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Uzume |title=Japanese Mythology A to Z |date=2009 |publisher=Infobase Publishing |isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="coulter-2013a">{{Cite book |last1=Coulter |first1=Charles Russell |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&dq=Ame-no-Uzume&pg=PT91 |title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities |last2=Turner |first2=Patricia |date=2013-07-04 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-135-96397-2 |language=en}}</ref> clan along with an order of female court and religious dancers. It was the origin of [[Kagura]] and [[Noh]]. Other descendants includes the Ujitoko clan from Ise province.
[[File:Sarutahiko Ōkami crop from Wittig collection.png|thumb|left|upright|Sarutahiko Ōkami; taken from a late-19th-century Japanese painting.]]
